speaker: interviewee
text: Tape four. OK, tape number four, with Mr. Isadore Horne, January 12, 1996. OK, you just met your future wife. Yeah, so I walked over. So the guys said to me, you go and talk to her. You Polack, go and talk to her. So I walked over and I said to her, listen, I'm a survivor, just like you. But I'm now in a uniform, dressed like an officer. You think I'm a Russian, but I'm not. I survived in the underground. I survived in the partisans. And I'm a Jewish man. I'm just like you-- I'm a survivor. You don't have to worry to talk with me. She felt entirely different when I told her all this stuff. And I say, I have for you a great idea. I say we are staying at the palace. And we have whatever this-- whatever your heart's desire. I'll take you over there. And I'll make you to be in charge of the kitchen, just like a manager. We have plenty Polkas-- Polish people. They walk over there in the kitchen and everything. You don't have to do nothing. But I want you to be there, and to relax. And-- and I'll help all the rest of the people, because I have food, and everything, whatever your heart's desire. And I'll give to you, and you'll give it to them. And they'll be able to come up a little bit-- because there were hungry. They didn't even have what to eat.

speaker: interviewer
text: She listened to me. And I took her over there. And she became, right away, over there-- the whole manager and everything-- and the general, they all know her. They know that she's my friend, you know, and things like this. And after a few weeks, I said to her-- do I talk quiet? Do I need louder to talk?

speaker: interviewer
text: No this is fine.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, that's fine? And I said to her, this-- the votes, I repeat what I said 51 years ago. I say, you don't have nobody in the whole world. All your family's gone. You told me all the stories. My family's gone. I was there. Everybody's gone. We don't have nobody. I don't-- let's get married, and let's build a future.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she says, OK. And that's the way-- and then I went to the general. She says the-- how will I stay with you in the-- it's a war. How will I travel with you? People don't have wives with them down in the front. I said, let me go and talk with the general.

speaker: interviewee
text: And me and the general, we were just like two brothers. And I told him the story, I'm going to get married. She don't-- he says, go ahead, do it. I'll give you a paper and that said that she'll be with you. We arranged a wedding just right away, shortly. And we made a wedding in [NON-ENGLISH]-- the wedding. Who was there, the few officers from the army. And the eight people for the survivors. I took enough champagne. It was-- it make you [INAUDIBLE], because I had plenty of that. And we got married. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: It was November?

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was November the 24th, 1944. And after the wedding the whole army, you know, that Isaac-- they all know me by the first name, because they used to come to buy all his things from the warehouse-- Isaac got married, Isaac [NON-ENGLISH], he got married. [LAUGHS] And I still didn't know, being there next to Warsaw, so close, was going on outside with the Jewish people, what happened, nothing. I didn't know nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: The only one thing I knew, until today, that the Russians could have got in to Warsaw, and take it over right away when I met, when I came in with the army. In that time, probably, I would say, maybe a few thousand Jewish people would have survived. Why? Because they were still in hiding, and there were still fighting with the partisans.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the Russia-- but the fights goes on, there for so many-- I think for four or five months, or six months, the Germans were fighting with the Poles. And the Russians didn't want to go in to help them. They didn't want to go, because they wanted-- they want that the Poles should come later, for mercy, to the Russians. Because they were afraid if they win something, they will have their own country back. That was the politic from Stalin. I'm sure about that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we-- so when Warsaw was liberated, and then of course things went fast. We got married November the 24th. And then, a few months later, Warsaw was freed. And then, later they-- they took over-- they went all the way to Germany. And they took over Berlin in a short time, too, and in the spring of 1945.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and-- so that's-- I mean, to tell you more, not necessarily, but how it went on, things, in the army, is not important.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when the war was over, we packed up our things and I was free to go home. So I decided where do I go? I don't have no home. Where will I go?

speaker: interviewee
text: The war is over. We are free. But we don't have anybody. We don't have no country. We don't have no land. We don't have no state. We don't have no home. We don't have nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we decided we're going to go to Lutsk, the city where I lived more or less next to mine. Mine was wiped out. Mine was burned down to the ground. There was nothing there left. Because I was there yet, I saw it. So Lutsk, is a city of Jewish people-- we'll see what happens. So we went to Lutsk. And in Lutsk was born our little girl, in Lutsk.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we stayed there, and then we see it's no place for us to go and live in Russia. I didn't want to stay and live in Russia. Even I had medals from the Russian army, you know, from my work, and this what I did. But I didn't want it. We say we'll go back. We'll go back from where we came. I mean we were so close to the German territory. You know-- and-- I mean, from Warsaw, over there to the Germans, so we want all the way to Russia. And then now, we went back. And we went back to go to the American zone in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we came with a little child, to travel. We finally travelled. We smuggled the borders here and there. And we came and we settled in Lodz. Lodz was the second largest city in Poland. But it was all gone. Everybody was killed. There was nothing there.

speaker: interviewee
text: We settled in Lodz, and I walked around on the street with a uniform. And the Jewish people were still scared at that time. And they used to say, [NON-ENGLISH], in Yiddish, and Jewish. And I knew Jewish. I understood what they talk. And they thought I'm a Russian officer. And they said, don't you worry, you see the Russian officers are walking around. They-- they will save us here. They will take care-- they will not let us to do anything. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Maybe I missed it earlier, what happened to your cousin Benjamin Feldman?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You kind of dropped him out of the story.

speaker: interviewee
text: I forgot to mention to you. While I was in kibbutz at the time, and I went to the hospital, they took him to the front, he was killed fighting.

speaker: interviewer
text: After all that.

speaker: interviewee
text: They told me because later the names, while I was in the hospital. The hospital saved me. That I didn't-- that they didn't take me on the front that time. Yeah, I forgot to tell you that.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, where are we now?

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, you had another, what, half year before you went to the DP camp with your wife.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah, but I finished you-- when we came to Lutsk.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right, right.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were over there. So we smuggled, we came over. And then we left Lodz, and we smuggled to the American Zone. Why? Because that time, while we lived in Lodz, they killed Jewish people in-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] oh, I forgot the name of the town.

speaker: interviewer
text: The pogrom in what-- '46 you mentioned.

speaker: interviewee
text: In '46 yeah. You must have this, I imagine.

speaker: interviewer
text: I wrote it down. I'll find it, keep going.

speaker: interviewee
text: You'll find it.

speaker: interviewer
text: I've got it here.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jewish people, refugees, came from-- because they survived in Russia during the war, came back home to Lodz. They took them, they pulled them out from the trains and killed them. When I heard this happened, I said, my God, let's run away from there. They killed millions of people, and even what is left, they're still going to kill, those anti-Semites-- the Poles?

speaker: interviewer
text: So we left and we came to the American Zone. We came to the American Zone. And we settled in a DP camp. The DP camp was something like-- ours was Foehrenwald, the name, not too far-- not too far from Munich. We-- we had what to eat, couldn't die over there.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we had like-- me and my wife, we had our one child, we had one room. We lived in the DP camp for three and a half years, long time. [SIGHS] After that, I got in touch with-- with a friend of mine. He was a [NON-ENGLISH] and a rabbi in Philadelphia. And he didn't know me, I didn't know him. But through the letters we got friendly, you know his family, and things like this. And he made it out papers for us. And we came over here. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And his name, by the way?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Potash.

speaker: interviewer
text: Potash.

speaker: interviewee
text: P-O-T-A-S-H. And we were very good friends through the years while we were in America. He moved later to Miami Beach, and we would visit him over there. We visited him in Israel. He has family in Israel, too. And we came here to America in the spring-- in the spring of 1949-- 1949. So more or less, you know, the years from-- from '46 to '49 was not excited to live in a DP camp. Because a DP camp was not a concentration camp-- you know, nobody killed us over there. But it was-- how does it say, [NON-ENGLISH]-- that means not dead, not alive. That's the way it was, the DP camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well we have a little time before the photographs and we meet your family. I thought we could spend just a little time-- did-- was there anything else you wanted to add?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, I just want to tell you that we came here to America, we settled in Pittsburgh. I went into the glass business. It was very tough, very hard in the beginning. No language, no car, no driving, nothing, just-- just I worked for somebody. It was in the glass business. And I saw what he does. So I figured what he does, I can do it, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I made myself cards with my name, America Mirror and Glass. And I went from door to door to sell glass and sell mirrors. And I saw that the people are not used to it for salesman to walk around and sell that stuff. Something else people used to sell, but not the glass and mirrors. But I see that everybody wants it and everybody gives me orders. So I came home and I said to my wife, what is it? Do they eat the glass? Seems like everybody likes glass. So I start working. And then later-- that was in-- I started in '49-- in 1950, I started already. In '51 I went and I took lessons to drive and somehow I managed to buy a little used pickup truck. And another year later, I started doing more bigger jobs. And I took some men to work.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I wind up later to buy a beautiful building from Bildo, which I used to do the work for in the glass. He used to build shopping centers. And I used to have like a secretary-- I couldn't handle-- and four people used to work for me. And I-- and we made a nice living. We-- then another probably-- but the children, my wife probably told you they were born in Pittsburgh. All the rest of the children. And beside being busy with a business and making a living, with my wife also gave work for the Jewish organizations like the Neighborhood Women. She was president. I don't know if she told you that from her-- from her interview. I became president from a lodge. And for years I was the chairman for the Adult Education Poland for the entire Neighborhood in Pittsburgh. And I became the council president for more 3,000 members, 14 lodges, and I was the president for the lodge. And I became active in the synagogue conservative, I was on the board. And thousands of people just walked out on the street always, hi, Izzy-- see, you knew me by the first name. Because I worked, and I find time. Being busy, I still have the time to go and do all those things. In 1978, in 1978, we decided that I would like to retire. I was only 62, but I figured I worked hard enough, I had enough. Maybe I deserve to relax. I can make a living not working. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So-- so I came-- so I had some friends in Los Angeles. So they asked us to come over there to visit them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we took a ride one time. And then they said to me, if you like to move to California, I'll give you a nice place-- San Diego, sunny San Diego. And they took us with their car, and we came here for a visit. And we visit San Diego. And we just fall in love with San Diego-- with San Diego. And I saw, I was in touch with a builder-- the house, you know? I saw this house right away. And then I said, my gosh, this house is worth 10 times as much as the house I have in Pittsburgh.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and we decided we were going to move. I sold my business. And I sold the house over there. Of course, for the house, I had another house over here. And I'm in [INAUDIBLE], living here in this house for the last 18 years. I became-- I did not rest here in San Diego. In the beginning, I thought maybe I will start working with the Neighborhood again. But then I find out there is a Holocaust Survivors Organization. I figured well, this is to my heart. A Holocaust organization is going to be more important than all the other organizations, because this is what I lived through and I should go. I-- I became a member right away. I became on the board for two years, and then they made me for vice president from 1987 to 19-- no, '86 until '89. And in '89 they elected me for president. After two years they reelect me again for president. I served the club for four years as president. I made a lot of tapes. I made a lot of interviews for the TV always. They used to come to my house, that bunch of cameras like this, as they used to tape me, all this, what I have to say because they knew that I'm the president of the Holocaust Survivors and they wanted to know all those things. And that's all I can tell you. At the conclusion, I have to say one word-- the Holocaust stays with us as long we live. For the last 52 years there isn't a day that we don't talk about the Holocaust among ourselves. We see a picture, whatever, we right away comes the horrible memories. We can never forget, and we will never forgive. And I recommend to everybody-- and I used to tell this always in my speeches when I used to talk to the new life club members-- we have to remember one thing, [NON-ENGLISH], in Hebrew means remember. [NON-ENGLISH], don't forget. Don't forget the murders the Nazis did to the Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: We cannot forget, never, never to forgive, and never to forget. Thank you, Mr. Horne.

speaker: interviewer
text: And getting up to speed. And--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my wife, Betty, the one I told you before. We got married 51 years ago, and we celebrated last year our 50th anniversary. On my right side is Gabi, our son-in-law. And his son, Avi, our grandson, and our daughter, Ronna. The one that they are not here is my son Harold David. Which he lives in Los Angeles. And another daughter--

speaker: interviewee
text: His wife.

speaker: interviewee
text: And his wife-- Connie. And here in San Diego, which she's not feeling well now. My other daughter, Sheree, and her husband, Bob Bendas, and Adam, his son, and the little girl, Julia. They are not here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Betty, was there anything else you wanted to add that we left off of your interview a couple hours ago?

speaker: interviewee
text: Are you talking to me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well I'm happy to have my husband here right beside me, and our older daughter and her husband and Avi. And I think if they are interested to say something, since they didn't hear what we were talking about, and just joined sort of in the family, and, Ronna?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, well of course we know the story of both our parents. And of course we grew up with no relatives. But we knew our parents were very special. They survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we came from a strong family. We love them very much for everything that they've done in their lives, and everything they've done for us in our lives. Avi?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm proud to tell my friends that my grandparents survived the Holocaust, and that they're really special to me, and I love them very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: What I can add to that is that we are all lucky that they survived because they are two very special people.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, that's good.

speaker: interviewee
text: Go ahead, Izzy, tell us about this one please.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh you want me to tell you something?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as I said before, my mother didn't have a husband-- I mean, my father died when I was three years old and I don't remember him. And she lived all her life working very hard in the house to support the kids and the farm. And the-- her name was Rachel, and Jewish. And the last name, of course, was Goldstein. Was our name was, in Europe, Goldstein. And the place was [POLISH], which was in Polish. And Jewish was [HEBREW]. It's a small village, was like a farm country. The date of the photo-- I don't remember the date. I don't know when this was taken.

speaker: interviewer
text: And go ahead Izzy, tell us about this photo.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my sister. Her name was Ritzi, and this is her husband, Sruelek. And they were married approximately, probably in 1935. And the picture was taken maybe a year later, '36, 1936. They lived in Lutsk, and then later they lived in the same place where I lived in the-- in the village.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK Izzy, tell us about this one.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my brother. His name is Altzei. He was older, much older than I, probably by eight, 10 years. And he was married. He had four children. He lived in the same place where we lived in the village. And he was killed with his wife and the children when my mother and all the rest of the people of the city were killed, same time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And go ahead Izzy, tell us about this one.

speaker: interviewee
text: His name is Chaim Yankl, the last name Feldman. He's the father, Benjamin's father-- the one who was hiding with me in the chimney-- that's his father. He was the one that I told you that he was killed on the way, walking, that he couldn't walk, and the Ukrainian policeman shot him right on that spot. He has another son in-- in Israel. He died just last year too-- two years.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, tell us about this one.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Benjamin Feldman. He was two years younger than I. He was with me in the chimney. We survived together in the woods. And he was killed in 1943. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] No, in 1944-- 1944 on the front.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, Izzy.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's me. Probably I was that time, around 20 years old. And this is neighbors of mine where I was a not too far I lived from them. I used to come on, be friendly with them. That's it. The date. The date was-- that was in Lutsk. That was in Lutsk. When I was that time in Lutsk, and I was given-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I was teaching Hebrew classes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, tell us who's in this photo.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is exactly the picture of the group that we are here now. From the right side it's Ronna, my daughter, me, my wife, Betty, and my son-in-law, Gabi, and my grandson, Avi. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] When this was taken, the place is here in San Diego. And date, I would say probably about a year ago. Yeah, about a year ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, Izzy, tell us about this picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is our wedding anniversary, our 50th wedding anniversary last year, November the 24th, 1994. And from the right it's Connie, our daughter-in-law, me, my wife, Betty, and my son, Harold. Which they do live in Los Angeles.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is also a picture from our 50th wedding anniversary from last year. From the right that's Bob, our son-in-law, and my wife, myself, my daughter, Sheree, the little one, Julia, and on the right side, Adam, grandson.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead please.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is our grandson Adam.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sheree's son.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sheree's son-- Bendas's son. The date-- the places, of course, all those pictures are from San Diego. The date of photo--

speaker: interviewee
text: Is last month isn't it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh last month, just a month ago.

speaker: interviewee
text: December '96.

speaker: interviewee
text: December 1996-- '95.

speaker: interviewee
text: Six.

speaker: interviewee
text: December is not in '95.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is our other grandson, Ronna's and Gabi's son, Avi, the one who was taking the picture here before. But this was taken probably too, about a year-- the last year.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Julia, our other daughter's, Sheree's-- Sheree's and Bob Bendas's daughter. She's four years old. It was taken just about a month ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, Izzy.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's me when I was 14 years old and I became bar mitzvah in my little village in Europe. How did I get a picture with me? I just had a very little, tiny little picture. And I used to carry this with me in my wallet all 30 years through the war. The picture was taken I guess-- I would say about 50 some years ago [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in my hometown.
